Our COO Mimi next year night. I love it. I don't care. I've got this feeling on the summer day when you were gone. I push my current to the front. Thanks, Layla. How cool is it? She's my sister. She is a co-CEO of this amazing business, and I was fortunate enough to be brought into it. And it's been a party. So I know that you guys are thinking, holy crap, that's a lot of cool culture bombs dropping all of her Layla isms. A lot of truth bombs that she likes to drop. But if you're focused on all the culture, how are you going to get everything else done? All the not sexy stuff. That's your operator. How many people out here identify as the operator? Raise your hands. All right, great. This is for you. If you do not have an operator, this is for the person you're looking for. Your role in a nutshell as the operator. Protect the business. Protect the entrepreneur. They are one in the same. If you protect the business, you're protecting your entrepreneur. If you're protecting your entrepreneur, you're protecting the business. They go hand in hand. They are not separate. The entrepreneur lives, breathes, eats with the business. So if you're protecting it, you're protecting him or her. Things you can do. Protecting their time and attention. Eliminate distractions. Personnel issues. Menial tasks. Dumb meetings and emails that they don't need to be on. Member complaints or conflicts. Get that out of their way. Don't have them distracted by that. That's your responsibility as an operator to take over and deal with that on your own. Allow them to pick what they're good at. Focus on one or two things. Is that sales? Is that marketing? Maybe it's both. Get a little crazy. But from there, take everything else off their plate. It's hard. It's going to be difficult. I cannot tell you how many times I have a conversation with Alex or Layla, and I say, stop doing that. They are not happy. They're like, you're taking my business away from me. Like, what am I supposed to do? Alex says, I'm bored. Which is scary, let me tell you. Alex's board is like my nightmare. But it makes their lives easier. And it's going to have to be a pull. You have to pull it off their plates. They're not going to push it to you. Some of the key responsibilities and differences between an entrepreneur and an operator. Alex and I like to joke that the entrepreneur is the gas, and the operator is the brakes. He is constantly pushing our business forward. Speed is king. Like, where do you think that came from? But the brakes, that's my job. I have to make sure that we're implementing correctly, and that we're slowing down and thinking about how we're executing on that speed. So the entrepreneur at the end of the day is responsible for designing the vision and the strategy behind the business. My job as the operator, ooh, either, is to be accountable for the implementation of that strategy and that vision. So the how. He acts as the face of the company, or she, as you see. Layla is now a lot more present in this business. Whereas the operator is liaising between the entrepreneur and the rest of the company. So communicating their vision down into tangible and executable tasks. The entrepreneur stays on top of trends, new marketing things, opportunities in the business, like new opportunities, basically. Whereas the operator is establishing policies, processes, frameworks, all the not sexy stuff for you entrepreneurs, but for an operator, if you're the right person, like me, you kind of enjoy that. Like I geek out over stuff like that. Alex looks at me as like you're crazy. Like I really enjoy writing a process. The entrepreneur is also responsible for constantly selling the company, the culture, the vision, and the why. He is at all times selling us on why we are doing what we are doing, reminding us about all of you out here, telling us, hey, you remember Joe's story, do you remember Sean's story? That's why you're getting up every morning. That's why we're doing this. This is our goal at the end of the day. That's his number one responsibility. For me, I'm looking at the risk management. What are we doing? And is it risk for our business? How can we negate that? Figuring out the how. One key concept, though, that both of us are responsible for is breaking the beliefs, pushing the limits, and demanding more out of our team. We have to be a team. We have to come together as a joint force when we're in front of the business and present ourselves as a united front. So one of the things I mentioned that the operator is responsible for is that they're accountable for implementation. This means you have an idea, or in this case Alex has an idea. And then we take that, we put it into a plan, and then we have an action after that. One of the biggest things that we do at GymLaunch is goal setting. And I think that you guys could really benefit from actually doing this. The number one thing that you do when you start goal setting is you define your what and your why. What is the result you want to achieve, and why is it important? Before you start anything else, defining these two things is critical. Otherwise, everything else you're putting together means nothing. Next, identify the who. What resources will you need to bring this idea to life? Think internally and externally. Be resourceful. Ha ha ha. Think about people you already have, people you could hire, vendors, contractors. Get creative, but think outside the box. Identifying the resources is really important, and you'll see why as we go along. Plan the when. You're going to set a target completion date. Outline the how. What action items have to happen along the way in order for us to get to that end goal? Who are the owners for each action item? How much time will each action item take to complete? And what's the due date for each action item? If you think about it at the end of the day, you're going to have all these steps along the way. Those are all of your action items that get you to the end result. Each of those could have different owners, could have different resources. They could take different amounts of time, and they should each have their own due date. Identify the risks. Okay. How many of you guys set a goal and you're like, oh yeah, we're going to get it done by March? It's going to be great. It's going to go smooth. We're going to have no issues. Let's be realistic. There are risks with every change and every decision you make. Identifying them and being proactive helps you prepare for them so that you don't feel like you're completely bombarded when something comes up. Prioritize. How high of a priority is this for your business? Alex brought this up earlier. You're taking all these notes, coming up with all these ideas. Really, how big of a priority is it? And lastly, revisit your target completion date. So is this really feasible? Looking at all of this information that you outlined, is that date that you said actually going to happen? Maybe it's not. Maybe you need to adjust it. That happens. So a former CEO of mine always told me, don't assume. When you assume, you make an asset of you and me. When he told me that, I was like completely shocked. I had said, well, I assumed that so and so knew this, this, and this. Well, when you assume, you're making an asset of both you and me. Wow, that'll put you in a place quick. So what I mean when I say this is, don't assume everybody gets from A to B the same way that you do. You have to think about it. As if you were giving someone a new workout instruction. You're going to walk them through every step of the way, right? Because they don't know what you know. They don't have the same information, the same knowledge, the same background, the same history. So when you're outlining what's happening in your next goal, don't assume they're going to get from one step to another and know that all of these things have to happen. It's a roadmap. If you're driving across country from here to Utah, or sorry, to Michigan, trying to think farther, you're going to have all these stops along the way. Obviously, that's the same situation that you're going to have when you're trying to get to an end goal. I wanted to show you guys, this is real life. This is one of my goals for this quarter, which is to hire a director of operations. Some of you have seen my posting out there. No, I'm not going anywhere. But there's a lot of work on my plate these days. And I need somebody who can take the day-to-day off my plate and help me so I can help Layla and Alex, and they can keep helping you guys even more. So I wrote up a quarterly goal. We have a template that my whole team uses. At first, they were, you know, a little resistant. But it actually makes the process much more streamlined. And because we have so many departments and different people, we're all using the same process and the same system. It's very, very systemized. It works well. We all know exactly what the expectations are. We'll put a copy of this up in the portal when we release all this from the event. But you can see that here, I outlined my quarterly goal is to hire a DOO. My target completion date is February 18th. I said that it's a high priority because it has a domino effect. So as I just said, I have a lot on my plate, which means that if I can't take more off of Layla and Alex's plates, then they can't do more for you guys. It has a direct relationship to how fast our business can grow. I think that's one of the highest priorities in our business right now, at least for me. My resources, obviously I need HR. I want Layla involved. I want her to scan everyone that's coming through, not only because she is our co-CEO, and she's got such a good pulse on our culture, because she knows me. I want whoever is being hired to pass her test, and to know that they passed her test means that they're probably going to be a great fit for me. I have the other directors involved. So I have a fantastic team of directors that run all of our different departments, sales, HR, customer service. I want them involved. This person is going to be with them every day helping them. They get to do a peer skill set interview. So two to three of them will interview each candidate that passes through our process and see if they're actually someone they want to work with. Would this person help me execute my goals and my projects across the business? I identified my potential roadblocks. Candidate flow is a real thing. I don't necessarily have a ton of control over that, but by accepting that it is a risk and a possible roadblock, I'm at least aware of it. The chosen candidate's availability and timeline to start. Some people can just drop what they're doing and start a new job. I personally needed a whole month before I could join the gym launch. That impacts when this person is going to start, so I need to be aware of that. And then I outlined the different action items that I thought were key pieces for me getting to my end goal. Again, I think this seems really elementary, and you guys are probably rolling your eyes like, obviously we do this, but do you actually write it down? Do you actually go through this? It's so important. The biggest thing that I like that we do here, you'll see at the bottom, is I will know I've succeeded when. So I will know I've succeeded when we've hired a DOO who's a great culture fit, as well as being driven, knowledgeable, and a high-level executor operator who can relieve much of my day-to-day activities. That is how I will know I've succeeded. And then I can close out the goal and move on to something else. All right, so let's say you guys actually did this with all of your goals. You wrote one of these out, and you're like, sweet, I'm going to get started. I'm going to go. No, you're not going to go. You're walking out of here with so many ideas. Your brain is probably exploding by the time you're leaving tomorrow. And you're like, holy crap, I can't wait to do all these things. I'm going to implement all this change, and we're going to hire all these people, and we're going to start the new culture thing, we're going to do core tenants. No. You need to set priorities. Setting priorities for yourself and the business is critical. If everything is important, then nothing is. If everything's on fire, then nothing is. How do you prioritize if everything's important? It's impossible. I've forced my team to prioritize all of their goals every quarter. They do not enjoy this. They cannot have two number ones. I don't care if it's similar departments, and it's all rolling up under one director. That one director needs to know what his or her focus is for the next 90 days. So this is an example where we have four goals, and we prioritize them. But you'll notice that the target completion dates don't tie directly to the priority. That's because priority does not equal completion date, and vice versa. There's something going on back here. So maybe, for instance, we're implementing a whole new CRM system. It's like a massive overhaul. It's going to impact our entire business, all of our billing, everything that we do. Probably the biggest priority of our company right now. It is also a very long process, long project, a lot of people involved, a lot to do. There's no way that's getting done completely by the end of this quarter. But it's going to be my number one. So that every time something about two, three, or four comes up, and I have work I need to do on number one, I will direct my attention to number one. It doesn't matter that I could get number two, three, or four done faster. That's not my priority. That's not getting me to the end goal and the biggest impact that we have on the business. Your priorities also define your resource allocation. People, time, money. If you have to make choices about those things, your priorities will tell you what those are. You'll know, okay, well, I can buy this because I'm not focused on that. And lastly, your priorities can and will change. All of this is great. You can plan. You can think that you know everything is going to happen. They're going to change. Something's going to happen. You're going to get hit with somebody's going to resign, or you're going to have way more leads than you expected, and your classes are going to expand, and it's going to create a higher urgency issue that you have to focus on. That's okay. That's real life. Plan for it. So once you've done these goals, and you're like, okay, great, who's going to own them? And how do I make sure my team is accountable for what they're owning? Collaboration. So you need to include all of your key stakeholders in the goal setting process. Don't just bark down at them that they need to take care of stuff. Bring them into it in the beginning. Tell them about the idea that you have, the end goal, and have them work through it with you. They're going to identify things that you're missing. Promise. Delegate. You cannot own everything. You will fail. Hand over the control. Edify your team. So let's say your operations manager is going to take over a really big goal that you have. Edify them in front of everyone else. It'll make them feel good. It'll make them feel like they have the power. It'll empower them to own it, to really take it where it needs to go. That's really important. A lot of times your operator is somebody who doesn't look for attention, but if they really like it and they need it, they're humans, they need a little bit of that boost each day. Encourage collaboration without you. So I know that this is going to be like a control thing where you're like, oh my gosh, I have to be in every meeting. I have to talk about this without everybody having to know exactly what's going on. Again, you will fail. You only have so much time. You can't be involved in everything. Encourage the people that are owning each goal to take time to work with the rest of the team. They might come up with some cool stuff without you there. Somebody might step up and become a leader because you're not there. A lot of times if you are there, you will hinder them from growing because they'll look to you out of default. So I think that's one of the first reports. We do these. I think it's okay and I think it's important to still know what's going on. You can do it as like a weekly check-in. So if you do a meeting or you do an end-of-week report, either way, just kind of get like an update on what's going on. We have a weekly deliverable section on our end-of-week report, and it identifies the pieces of each quarterly goal that the team is working on. It just tells me where they're at, what they finished this week, what they're working on in the next few blocks, and what they're working on next week. Nine times out of ten, I have no feedback from them. But it's good to know where they're at. If you have like a weekly leadership check-in, so we have a Monday strategic meeting where all the directors and all of our EAs and Alice and Layla, we all join into a meeting. In those calls, we update each other on it so that everyone knows what's going on. Common reasons for failure. So again, realistically, something's going to fail. Just come to terms with it. Biggest reasons are not people, necessarily. It's more that you didn't prepare the right way. So if they don't have a clear or strong why behind what they're doing, this could be a big reason for failure. Lack of resources. Did you over-allocate? Did you give one person seven things to do and complete while they still have their normal job every day? That's not feasible. They're going to fail. Unclear priorities. So earlier I talked about how important it was to set priorities. Again, like I said, if they don't know what their priorities are, then they don't know where to focus their time and attention. And if one thing has to drop, they don't know which one to drop. The goal wasn't smart. So for us, nerdy operators, a lot of us know what smart goals are. But for those of you that don't, a smart goal means it's specific, measurable, it's achievable, it's relevant, and it's time-bound. Lastly, fear of change. People are afraid of change. No matter how much you say you're not, you are. So how do you communicate change? Why is change so hard? Because the status quo is comfortable. People like to be comfortable. And humans naturally avoid discomfort. Therefore, humans naturally avoid change. Big things happen when you do the little things right. I think this is extremely important when communicating change. I've seen several of you posting in the groups about, oh, I'm changing my pricing, oh, I let someone go, and I don't know how to tell my members, I'm changing my billing cycle. That's a common one. So you're going to be in a position to communicate the change the right way, nine times out of ten, it's going to be successful. It's not going to be stressful, and you're going to feel prepared communicating it. You're going to feel more conviction communicating it because you did these little things in advance. The five W's. The what, the why, the who, the when, and the where. So these five W's represent the most basic questions that people will have about the change and how it will be impacting them. By proactively answering these in writing, that is extremely important, in writing, it will help the process go much smoother. I say in writing, because if you're saying, oh yeah, I do this, don't worry, but you do it in your head, you're missing something. Writing it down is proven to actually have a better impact, and you will actually be more successful if you write down every answer that you have. So the what, what exactly is changing? Are we moving locations? Are we reorganizing? Are we changing pricing? Make this as clear, detailed, and to the point as possible. Why? Why do we have to change? So if we go back and we think about how people are naturally avoiding change, they want to know why we have to. What problem is this change solving? Why is this a benefit to the audience? So I say audience, because it's important to stop and think about who your target audience is when you're building this communication. It could be internal, it could be external, it could be both. You need to make sure that there's a clear message that resonates with whomever is being impacted and who you are communicating to. Who? Who is being impacted by the change, both internally and externally? Are all employees being affected similarly? Or maybe just the traders? Maybe just the ops team? It could be a different communication method that you need for each of them, because it has a different impact on each of them. Remember to look at this from all avenues. Bring in others. Go back to the collaboration. Bring in your ops manager. Bring in other people who represent the different groups. See if you're missing something. When? So when is this change going to occur? What's a realistic timeline for this to be released? This really helps people prepare so they aren't off guard. Most of the time people are resistant to change because they feel like it just happened. And they couldn't get their minds around it. They're like, oh my gosh, they're doing this? And I didn't know what to do in advance. And they didn't tell me, if you tell them even a day in advance, you're doing yourself a service. If you don't know exactly when it's going to change, you have an overall timeline, but then set expected dates when you will know more information so that they understand and they know what to look forward to. And then just communicate with them along the way. If things change, let them know. Let them know why. Be real. I know that in the gym world, this isn't always feasible. It's like things go quickly. I mean, gym launch goes quickly too. But if you can take even just a few minutes to try to follow these steps and do the best you can, you're still doing better than you were before. Where? This is when I think a lot of people miss. So where do we need to make updates? Where do we communicate this? Where can the audience find this and more information about the change? So think both internally and externally again. Do you have a Facebook members group? Do you have an employee bulletin board? Those are different areas that you might want to post about this change. Point and any questions or concerns to a certain place. So for instance, if you are changing membership pricing and you're announcing this change to your employees first, perhaps, and they have questions or concerns, always wrap up your communication by saying, if you have any questions or concerns, please go here. It could be an email address. It could be a timeframe or a certain person. Give them an outlet so that you are not bombarded immediately after this presentation with all sorts of questions and concerns. Like, they need to feel like they have a place to go and that they're not just left hanging if they do have questions. That makes them feel more uncertain than they did before you walked in. Nine times out of ten. If you follow this process, you're going to find a hole in your plan. You could think of something really awesome to add before you actually roll it out. You could realize that your why no longer makes sense or it's missing. Things change. Your why could have changed. You're going to learn more about your employees and your customers by training yourself to think like them. If you're proactively thinking, how would this impact Joe? Let's say you use your head trainer. Then you're thinking about him and you're learning about him more. If you're proactively thinking about your customers and wondering what questions they're going to ask when you roll this out, before you roll it out, you're learning more about them. You are trying to understand them and you become a better leader that way. You're going to experience less resistance to the change. Ultimately, that's the end goal, right? So if you follow these steps, I highly recommend, then it should go smoother. Ultimately, you're going to save time and energy. It seems like work to put in in advance, but you have to put in the work for its payoff in the end. If you're not scrambling to deal with after-questions and after-thoughts, and oh, I missed this, I missed that, oh crap, I forgot about these people, because you did all the work in advance, then I say you're going to tell a huge head over. It's not sexy at all. But for me, I enjoy it. And I'm hoping that you guys got some good nugget out of this and that you walk away with some good road maps to communicate change, to setting goals, everything in your business. And as Alex says, do the mornship, do it consistently, yield unborn results. Thank you. Thank you.
